message.content: Adversaries may exploit Windows safe mode to disable endpoint defenses, leveraging the limited set of drivers and services that start in this mode. Safe mode, including its variant with networking, often prevents third-party security software like EDR tools from launching. Attackers can force a system into safe mode by altering Boot Configuration Data (BCD) stores, which manage boot settings. They may also modify Registry values to ensure their malicious applications and services start in safe mode, or register malicious COM objects to load during this state. This technique can significantly impair defenses, leaving systems vulnerable to further exploitation. Notably, the REvil ransomware group has been observed using this method to reboot systems into safe mode with networking. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting changes to BCD stores and Registry keys, ensuring security software can operate in safe mode, and employing robust logging and alerting mechanisms to detect and respond to unauthorized modifications.
